candidate_problems.text: How can the conjecture non-arbitrarily define the boundary and level of description at which a subsystem counts as an 'agent' with genuine causal authorship, rather than a merely convenient coarse-graining? The open problem is to specify principled criteria for agent individuation, level selection, and stable partitions of state space so that claims about free will are not observer-relative.

What exact measurable invariant distinguishes 'self-model-mediated dynamics' from sophisticated but unfree control? The conjecture gestures at intervention-sensitive internal variables, goals, predictions, and error-correction, but it remains unresolved which formal metrics—e.g. integration, counterfactual richness, controllability, model-based planning, temporal depth—are necessary or sufficient for freedom.

Does conditional 'could have done otherwise' capture the normative core of free will, including responsibility and authorship, or does it only redescribe causal dependence? The unresolved tension is whether intervention-sensitive internal dynamics are enough to ground the stronger philosophical notions people care about, or whether the conjecture explains agency while leaving moral responsibility untouched.
